title: Spotify — Audio Streaming — System Design
description: Design a music streaming service: stream audio with instant start across a huge catalog, manage personal libraries/playlists, and support offline listening. The themes are segmented audio delivery via CDN, the catalog/library split, offline download/sync, and play-event signals feeding recommendations and royalties.

nodes.label: Music Client
nodes.description: Streams audio, manages playlists, downloads for offline.

nodes.label: API Gateway
nodes.description: Routes playback, library, and search requests.

nodes.label: Streaming Service
nodes.description: Authorizes playback and issues signed segment URLs.

nodes.label: Audio CDN
nodes.description: Serves audio segments near listeners; the hot read path.

nodes.label: Library / Playlist Service
nodes.description: User playlists, saved tracks, and library state.

nodes.label: Library Store
nodes.description: Per-user playlists and saved library, sharded by user.

nodes.label: Ingestion / Transcode
nodes.description: Encodes uploaded masters into streaming bitrates + segments.

nodes.label: Play Event Log
nodes.description: Play/skip events for recommendations, royalties, and resume.

nodes.label: Entitlement / License Service
nodes.description: Authorizes playback/download: subscription, region, track availability, device; issues DRM/offline licenses.

nodes.label: Rights / Policy Store
nodes.description: Per-track licensing windows, regional availability, takedowns; per-user subscription/device state.

nodes.label: Search Index
nodes.description: Inverted index over catalog for prefix/typo-tolerant search and ranking; rebuildable from the catalog.

nodes.label: Royalty Accounting
nodes.description: Idempotent consumer that dedupes play events and writes billable-stream counts to the ledger.

nodes.label: Feature / History Store
nodes.description: Per-user listening history and model features feeding online recommendation retrieval.

functional: Search and browse a large music catalog (tracks, albums, artists).
Stream a track with near-instant start and adaptive quality.
Create and manage playlists; save tracks to a library.
Personalized home and generated playlists from listening history.
Play/download only what the user is licensed for (subscription, region, availability).
nonFunctional: Low start latency (first audio in well under a second).
Read-heavy streaming served globally via CDN.
Durable audio storage; catalog/library metadata consistent.
Offline listening works without connectivity and re-syncs later.
Capture play events reliably (for recommendations and royalties).
Royalty counts are accurate and auditable (deduped, fraud-filtered).

capacity.label: Catalog storage
capacity.note: A 3.5-min track at 96/160/320 kbps + lossless is ~10–40 MB across the ladder; 100M tracks ≈ several PB of durable object storage (drives object-store layout, step 7).

capacity.label: Concurrent streams
capacity.note: At 160 kbps ≈ 20 KB/s per stream, 30M streams ≈ 600 GB/s edge egress — must be CDN-served, never origin (step 2).

capacity.label: Segment QPS
capacity.note: 10-sec segments × 30M streams ≈ 3M GETs/sec at the edge. At a 98–99% CDN hit ratio the origin sees ~30–60k GETs/sec — the origin-miss budget that justifies prewarming hot releases (steps 2, 7).

capacity.label: Start latency
capacity.note: Prefetch segment 1 (~one 10-sec chunk, ~200 KB at 160 kbps) + small buffer; p99 well under a second (step 2).

capacity.label: Play events
capacity.note: Play/skip/seek/progress events; ~30-day raw retention, then aggregated. Partition the log by user/track; size consumers for recs + royalties separately (steps 6, 7).

capacity.label: Catalog / search reads
capacity.note: Browse + playlist hydration + search; cache-heavy. Search runs against an inverted index, not the catalog DB (step 3).

capacity.label: Ingestion
capacity.value: ~60k new tracks/day, transcode each into the bitrate ladder
capacity.note: Async transcode + segment + publish; sized so a label drop never affects playback (step 7).

api.description: Authorize playback (subscription + region + track availability + device) and return the manifest with signed, expiring segment URLs and a DRM license.
api.response: { "manifestVersion": 3, "manifest": "...", "bitrates": [96, 160, 320], "segmentUrlTtl": 300, "license": { "keyId": "k_2", "expiresAt": "..." } }  (403 if not entitled / unavailable in market)

api.description: Prefix/typo-tolerant search over tracks, albums, and artists via the search index (not the catalog DB), ranked by relevance + popularity, filtered to the user's market.
Add/move/remove playlist items. Position is explicit; an If-Match version enables optimistic concurrency for collaborative edits.
api.response: { "itemId": "pi_12", "position": "...", "version": 42 }  (409 on version conflict → client re-reads + retries)

api.description: Pull library changes since a cursor (playlists, saved tracks, edits) for incremental offline reconciliation.
Authorize an offline download of a track/playlist and issue an offline DRM license bound to the device, subject to subscription/region/device-limit checks.
api.response: { "downloadId": "d_1", "tracks": 24, "offlineLicense": { "expiresAt": "...", "revalidateEvery": "30d" } }  (403 if device limit reached / not entitled)

api.description: Report play/skip/progress events in a batch (offline plays flush on reconnect). Event IDs make ingestion idempotent so royalties don't double-count.

dataModel.name: tracks (catalog)
dataModel.note: Authoritative track metadata + audio asset references.

dataModel.name: artists / albums (catalog)
dataModel.note: Browse hierarchy; denormalized into the search index for ranking.

dataModel.name: rights_policy
dataModel.note: Owns 'can this user play/download this track here now?' — checked by the entitlement service.

fields.name: version
fields.type: bigint (bumped on change → invalidate manifests/offline licenses)

dataModel.name: subscriptions / devices
dataModel.note: Per-user entitlement state and registered devices (offline device limit).

fields.name: devices
fields.type: list<{ device_id, last_seen }> (max N for offline)

dataModel.name: playlist_items
dataModel.note: One row per playlist entry (not a single track_ids array), so edits/reorders are concurrent-safe.

dataModel.name: playlists / saved_library
dataModel.note: Playlist header + per-user saved tracks; versioned for optimistic concurrency and sync.

dataModel.name: offline_download
dataModel.note: Per-device offline state machine (intent → cached → license).

dataModel.name: play_event (raw)
dataModel.note: Append-only stream record; deduped downstream by event_id.

fields.name: ms_played
fields.type: int (counts as a stream past a threshold, e.g. 30s)

dataModel.name: royalty_ledger (accounting output)
dataModel.note: Idempotent royalty consumer's authoritative billable-stream counts.

dataModel.name: feature_store (rec output)
dataModel.note: Per-user history/features for online recommendation retrieval.

patterns.name: Segmented audio + CDN
patterns.what: Encode tracks into bitrates and small segments served from CDN for instant start and adaptive quality.
patterns.whenToUse: Streaming media with global read fanout.

patterns.name: Catalog/library split
patterns.what: Separate the shared immutable catalog from per-user mutable library/playlists.
patterns.whenToUse: Shared content + personal collections with different scaling.

patterns.name: Search index off the catalog
patterns.what: Maintain a rebuildable inverted index, fed from the catalog and ingestion, for prefix/typo-tolerant ranked search separate from the authoritative metadata DB.
patterns.whenToUse: Large catalogs where users search/browse by relevance, not exact id.

patterns.name: Reference-by-id playlists
patterns.what: Playlists store track ids; audio lives once and is referenced by many.
patterns.whenToUse: Same track in millions of playlists.

patterns.name: Offline download + sync
patterns.what: Cache encrypted audio locally; reconcile library changes on reconnect.

patterns.name: Per-play entitlement + DRM
patterns.what: Authorize each play/download against subscription, region, and track availability, then issue short-TTL DRM/offline licenses; a versioned rights policy invalidates on takedown.
patterns.whenToUse: Licensed media where access depends on user, place, device, and time.

patterns.name: Play-event stream
patterns.what: Stream play/skip events for recommendations, royalties, and resume.
patterns.whenToUse: Behavior that drives personalization and accounting.

patterns.name: Idempotent royalty ledger
patterns.what: Dedupe play events by id, filter fraud, apply the stream threshold, and write an auditable per-track ledger — isolated from recommendations.
patterns.whenToUse: Money/accounting derived from an at-least-once event stream.

patterns.name: Async ingestion pipeline
patterns.what: Encode uploaded masters into streaming assets off the serving path.
patterns.whenToUse: Adding content to the catalog without blocking playback.

steps.title: 1. Naive: Stream the Whole File from the App Server
description: Start with the simplest thing that works. Each track is stored as a single encoded audio file in the object store. To play, the client asks the streaming service, which reads the whole file from storage and streams the bytes back itself — one bitrate, one origin, no CDN, no segmentation.

This is correct and easy to reason about — and it exposes the problems the rest of the design solves. First, the client can't start playing until enough of one big file arrives from a single origin, so start latency is poor and gets worse the farther the listener is. Second, every concurrent stream hits the app server / origin, so tens of millions of listeners overwhelm it — there's nothing serving audio near the user. Third, one fixed bitrate can't adapt to a weak connection, causing stalls.

So the baseline motivates everything that follows: encode tracks into multiple bitrates, split them into small segments, and serve those segments from a CDN with a manifest for instant, adaptive start.

view.caption: The music client calls the API gateway into the streaming service, which reads the whole audio file from the object store and streams it back.

steps.decisionPrompt: If the app server reads a whole track from the origin and streams it itself, what happens to start latency and to the origin at tens of millions of concurrent streams?
whyNow: Establishing the naive baseline first makes it obvious why audio must be segmented, bitrate-encoded, and served from a CDN — each later mechanism removes a specific pain from here (slow start, origin overload, no adaptation).

recap.after: One audio file per track, streamed from the app server / origin at a fixed bitrate.
recap.newRisk: Slow start over a single origin, no CDN to absorb concurrent streams, and no adaptive bitrate for weak connections.

traps.trap: Streaming a whole single-bitrate file from the app server.
traps.why: Start is slow, the origin can't serve tens of millions of concurrent streams, and a fixed bitrate stalls on poor connections.
traps.instead: Segment bitrate-encoded audio and serve segments from a CDN behind a manifest, prefetching the first segment for instant start.

steps.title: 2. Segmented Audio Delivery via CDN
description: Audio is encoded (during ingestion) into a few bitrates and split into small segments. Playback returns a manifest of segment URLs; the client fetches the first segment immediately and buffers a little, giving near-instant start, then adapts bitrate to the connection.

Serve segments from a CDN, not the origin store — that's how ~30M concurrent streams (~600 GB/s, ~3M segment GETs/sec) are handled, with audio cached near listeners. The streaming service only authorizes playback and hands out signed, expiring segment URLs (short TTL, e.g. 5 min) plus a DRM license; it does not stream bytes itself. Before issuing the manifest it calls the entitlement service to confirm subscription, market availability, and track licensing — playback authorization is on the critical path, not an afterthought.

Segment objects are immutable and cached at the edge with long TTLs keyed by track+bitrate+segment; the manifest carries a version so a takedown or rights change can invalidate it. At a 98–99% CDN hit ratio the origin object store sees only ~30–60k GETs/sec — the origin-miss budget that step 7's prewarming protects.
Tracks are far smaller than video, so the pipeline is lighter — but the structure (encode -> segment -> CDN) mirrors any media-streaming design.

view.caption: The streaming service checks entitlement (subscription/region/availability), then hands the client signed segment URLs, so the client pulls audio segments from the CDN, which origin-fetches from the object store that ingestion fills.

options.name: Segmented audio served from a CDN (default)
options.description: Tracks are transcoded at ingest into a few bitrates and split into small segments; playback returns a manifest of signed segment URLs that clients fetch from edge CDN nodes.
pros: Tracks are pre-segmented and cached at edge CDN nodes, so playback starts in well under a second near the listener.
Tens of millions of concurrent streams hit the CDN, not the origin object store — the origin only serves cache misses.
Adaptive bitrate per segment handles flaky networks without re-fetching the whole file.
cons: CDN egress is a major recurring cost at this scale.
Requires an ingestion step to transcode and segment every track into multiple bitrates.
Signed segment URLs, DRM key rotation, and the entitlement check add auth complexity on the playback path (cache entitlement results to keep start latency low).

view.caption: Segmented delivery: the streaming service checks entitlement, issues signed URLs, and the client fetches segments from the CDN, with the object store as origin and ingestion storing encoded audio.

options.name: Peer-to-peer assisted delivery
options.description: Listeners fetch popular segments from nearby peers to offload bandwidth, with the CDN and origin as fallback when peering is unavailable.
pros: Listeners fetch popular segments from nearby peers, offloading bandwidth from the CDN/origin (Spotify's original approach).
Hot new releases scale with the audience since each listener is also a seeder.
Reduces CDN egress cost for the long tail of concurrent popular tracks.
cons: Mobile and NAT'd clients can't reliably peer, so you still need CDN fallback everywhere.
Peer availability is unpredictable, hurting the sub-second start-latency guarantee.
Sharing licensed audio between clients raises DRM and content-protection concerns; largely abandoned in favor of cheap CDN.

view.caption: Peer-assisted delivery: the client fetches some segments from nearby peer clients and the rest from the CDN, which origin-fetches from the object store.

options.name: Stream whole files from origin (no CDN)
options.description: Each play streams the entire track file directly from the object store with no segmentation, transcoding, or edge caching.
pros: Simplest pipeline: no transcoding, segmenting, or edge caching to operate.
One copy of each track in the object store, no cache invalidation.
Adequate for a tiny catalog with few concurrent listeners.
cons: Start latency is high — the client waits on a full-file fetch from a distant origin.
Tens of millions of concurrent streams would crush the origin object store's bandwidth.
No adaptive bitrate, so poor networks stall or fail entirely.

view.caption: Origin streaming: the streaming service reads whole audio files straight from the object store with no CDN in between.

steps.decisionPrompt: How do you make a track start playing in under a second from a 100M-track catalog?

concepts.definition: Bitrate-encoded, segmented audio served from CDN edge with a manifest.
concepts.whyItMatters: Instant start, adaptive quality, and massive read fanout without touching the origin.
concepts.example: Prefetch segment 1 at 160kbps; switch to 320 if bandwidth allows.

whyNow: Playback is the headline experience; CDN-served segments are how streaming scales and start latency is met.

recap.newRisk: Finding and organizing tracks (catalog + personal library) is a separate concern.

flows.name: Play: authorize → manifest → CDN segments
flows.note: Streaming service checks entitlement (subscription/region/availability), then returns signed segment URLs + a DRM license; the client fetches the first segment from the nearest CDN edge for instant start.

traps.trap: Treating playback authorization as a one-time login check, not a per-play decision.
traps.why: Whether a user can play a track depends on subscription tier, market, and the track's licensing/takedown state right now — all of which change. Skipping the per-play entitlement check ships plays that violate licensing.
traps.instead: Check entitlement (subscription/region/availability) on each play before issuing the manifest, cache the result briefly per (user, track), and bind a short-TTL DRM license and signed segment URLs to that decision.

traps.trap: Sizing the origin object store for the stream count instead of the cache-miss count.
traps.why: At ~3M segment GETs/sec, sending even a few percent to origin is tens of thousands of GETs/sec; a new release with a cold cache can become an origin miss-storm that starves real misses.
traps.instead: Design to a target hit ratio (≥98–99%), prewarm the CDN for big drops, and use an origin shield / collapsed forwarding so concurrent misses for the same segment hit origin once.

failureDrills.scenario: A new album drops and the edge cache is cold — millions request its segments at once.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The first request per segment fills the edge; concurrent misses are collapsed so origin sees one fetch per segment, not millions. Start latency briefly rises then recovers as the edge warms.
failureDrills.mitigation: CDN prewarm before the announced drop, origin shield / request collapsing, and per-track popularity-aware caching (see step 7).

failureDrills.scenario: A CDN region degrades or an edge POP goes down mid-stream.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Clients fail over to the next-nearest edge using alternate manifest URLs; playback rebuffers briefly rather than stopping. The origin is shielded by the remaining edges.
failureDrills.mitigation: Multi-CDN / multi-POP manifest URLs, client-side retry with backoff, and a small client buffer to ride out a failover.

steps.title: 3. Catalog vs Library Split
description: Two very different data shapes: the catalog is huge, shared, and mostly immutable (tracks/albums/artists); the library is per-user, small, and mutable (saved tracks, playlists). Keep them in separate services/stores so each scales on its own terms.

The catalog DB is the authoritative metadata store, but it is the wrong tool for search: prefix/typo-tolerant queries and relevance ranking over ~100M tracks need an inverted index, not LIKE scans. So search/browse runs against a separate search index fed from the catalog (and from ingestion as new tracks land), ranked by relevance + popularity and filtered to the user's market. The catalog DB stays the source of truth; the index is rebuildable derived state.

The library service owns per-user state, sharded by user. Audio assets are referenced from the catalog; neither service stores audio bytes. This split mirrors the e-commerce catalog pattern: a shared read-optimized catalog plus per-user mutable state, with search as its own read path.

view.caption: The gateway routes browse to the catalog service over the catalog store, search to the catalog service over the search index, and playlist/library calls to the library service over the library store.

steps.decisionPrompt: Why separate the shared music catalog from each user's playlists/library?

concepts.definition: Shared immutable catalog separate from per-user mutable library state.
concepts.whyItMatters: Each scales differently — catalog is cacheable/read-heavy; library shards by user.
concepts.example: One 'Bohemian Rhapsody' track; millions of playlists reference its id.

concepts.definition: An inverted index, fed from the catalog, serving prefix/typo-tolerant ranked search separately from the authoritative metadata store.
concepts.whyItMatters: Relevance ranking and fuzzy/prefix matching over 100M tracks can't come from row lookups; search is its own read path and data pipeline.
concepts.example: Typing 'bohem' returns 'Bohemian Rhapsody' ranked by popularity, filtered to the user's market.

whyNow: The catalog/library split organizes the metadata side and sets up playlists as references — the natural structure for a music app. Search gets its own index here because 'search a large catalog' is a core requirement that a metadata DB can't serve well.

traps.trap: Serving search with LIKE / full-table scans against the catalog DB.
traps.why: Prefix and typo-tolerant matching, relevance + popularity ranking, and ~100k searches/sec over 100M rows will overwhelm the authoritative store and still rank poorly.
traps.instead: Maintain a dedicated inverted index (rebuildable from the catalog, updated by ingestion) and treat search as a separate, cacheable read path with its own ranking.

recap.after: Searchable shared catalog (index + browse) + per-user library, cleanly separated.
recap.newRisk: Playlists referencing tracks must be efficient and not duplicate audio/metadata.

steps.title: 4. Playlists and Library (Reference by ID)
description: Playlists reference tracks by id — never copies of audio or full metadata. A track appears in millions of playlists but exists once; the playlist stores references and hydrates track metadata (and audio manifest) on read. Model each entry as a playlist_item row with an explicit position (a fractional/lexo rank), not a single track_ids array — so an insert or reorder touches one row instead of rewriting the whole list, and concurrent edits don't clobber each other.

The library service handles add/remove/reorder on the owner's shard and returns fast. Edits carry a playlist version (optimistic concurrency): a stale edit gets a 409 and the client re-reads and retries. Collaborative playlists are then just multi-writer ordered lists — fractional ranks let two people insert at the same spot without a global lock; ties break deterministically.

Reads hydrate playlist track ids against the catalog (cached) for display. Hydration must handle a track that is now deleted, taken down, or region-restricted — it renders as unavailable rather than breaking the playlist or leaving a dangling reference. Hot tracks (in many popular playlists) are a catalog read hotspot, so hydration reads through a cache.

view.caption: The client manages playlists through the library service backed by the library store, with the catalog service resolving track details.

options.name: Playlists reference tracks by id (default)
options.description: A playlist stores only an ordered list of track ids; reads hydrate those ids against the catalog to fetch each track's metadata and audio manifest.
pros: A track in 10M playlists is stored once in the catalog; playlists hold only ordered track ids.
Editing track metadata (title, art) updates everywhere instantly because there's a single canonical copy.
Playlist rows stay tiny, so user libraries are cheap to store and sync.
cons: Rendering a playlist requires a join/fan-out to fetch each referenced track's metadata.
A deleted or region-restricted track leaves a dangling reference to handle gracefully.
Hot tracks become a read hotspot in the catalog under popular playlists.

view.caption: Playlists store only track ids in the library store, and the library service resolves them against the catalog service on read.

options.name: Denormalize track metadata into each playlist
options.description: Each playlist embeds a full copy of every track's metadata inline, so rendering needs no catalog join but copies must be kept in sync on edits.
pros: Reading a playlist is a single fetch — title, artist, and art are already inline, no catalog join.
Playlist rendering is fast and resilient to catalog latency or partial outages.
Simple read path for offline use where the catalog isn't reachable.
cons: A track in 10M playlists duplicates its metadata 10M times — large storage blow-up.
A metadata edit (e.g. corrected title) must fan out to every playlist copy, or copies drift.
Region restrictions and takedowns are hard to enforce across millions of stale copies.

view.caption: Denormalized: track metadata is copied from the catalog into each playlist row in the library store, so reads need no catalog lookup.

steps.decisionPrompt: A track is in 10M playlists. How do you store playlists without duplicating the track?
whyNow: Reference-by-id is the efficient playlist model and reuses the same 'store once, reference by id' idea as feeds — a clean reuse.

concepts.definition: Store ids in the collection; hydrate full objects on read.
concepts.whyItMatters: Bounds storage and makes shared items (tracks) editable in one place.
concepts.example: Playlist = [t_9, t_42, ...]; metadata + audio hydrated at read time.

concepts.definition: Each item carries a fractional position; edits carry a version so a stale write is rejected (409) and retried.
concepts.whyItMatters: Inserts/reorders touch one row, and collaborative edits converge without a global lock.
concepts.example: Two users insert between items ranked 1.0 and 2.0 → 1.5 and 1.75; versions catch a stale overwrite.

traps.trap: Storing the playlist as one ordered track_ids array updated in place.
traps.why: Every insert/reorder rewrites the whole array, two concurrent edits last-write-wins over each other, and large playlists make every edit expensive.
traps.instead: Model items as rows with a fractional/lexo rank and version the playlist for optimistic concurrency; reorders and collaborative edits touch one row and converge.

traps.trap: Assuming every referenced track is always playable at hydration time.
traps.why: Tracks get taken down or become region-restricted after they're added; a naive join leaves a dangling/broken entry.
traps.instead: Hydrate through the catalog (cached) and the rights policy, rendering removed/unavailable tracks as greyed-out 'unavailable' rather than failing the whole playlist.

recap.after: Efficient reference-based playlists (item rows + versioning) hydrated on read, unavailable tracks handled.
recap.newRisk: Users want music without connectivity — offline download and sync.

steps.title: 5. Offline Download and Sync
description: Offline is a state machine, not just 'download files.' A download moves through pending → downloaded → (expired | revoked). On request the library service first checks entitlement (subscription, market, and the per-device offline limit), then issues an offline DRM license bound to the device with an expiry; only then does the client fetch and cache the chosen-bitrate segments into an encrypted local store so content isn't freely extractable.

Library edits made offline (new playlist, saved track, reorder) buffer locally as operations and reconcile on reconnect against a sync cursor — pull changes since the last cursor, replay local ops, and resolve conflicts (playlists are versioned ordered lists, so concurrent edits merge via fractional ranks rather than clobbering). Play events recorded offline also buffer and flush (step 6).

The offline license must be revalidated periodically (e.g. every 30 days online) and on reconnect: if the subscription lapsed, the market changed, or the track was taken down, the local copy is expired/revoked and made unplayable. Downloads survive short offline windows but can't outlive entitlement.

view.caption: For offline, the client requests a download via the library service, which checks entitlement and issues an offline license; the client then pulls and caches encrypted audio segments from the CDN (origin-fetched from the object store) onto the device.

steps.decisionPrompt: A user downloads a playlist on wifi and listens on a plane. What's stored locally, and how does the library stay in sync?

concepts.definition: Encrypted local audio cache + buffered library edits synced on reconnect.
concepts.whyItMatters: Enables connectivity-free listening while keeping the library consistent.
concepts.example: Download 24 tracks; add a playlist offline; both reconcile when back online.

whyNow: Offline is the audio-specific differentiator (smaller files make it practical); the download + sync model is the key design point.

recap.after: Encrypted offline cache + library sync on reconnect.
recap.newRisk: Plays must be captured (offline too) for recommendations and royalties.

flows.name: Download for offline; reconcile on reconnect
flows.note: Library checks entitlement and issues an offline license, the client caches encrypted segments from the CDN; offline library edits buffer locally and sync against a cursor when back online.

messages.label: track ids + segment manifest + offline license
sync buffered library edits + re-validate licenses

traps.trap: Caching audio offline without a device-bound, expiring license.
traps.why: A perpetual local copy ignores subscription lapses, market changes, and takedowns — the user keeps playing content they're no longer licensed for, and rights holders aren't paid.
traps.instead: Issue an offline DRM license bound to the device with an expiry and periodic revalidation; expire/revoke the local copy when entitlement changes or the track is taken down.

traps.trap: Reconciling offline library edits by last-write-wins on the whole playlist.
traps.why: Two devices that edited the same playlist offline overwrite each other on reconnect, silently losing edits.
traps.instead: Buffer edits as operations, sync against a per-user cursor, and merge versioned ordered lists via fractional ranks so concurrent inserts/reorders converge.

failureDrills.scenario: A user edits a collaborative playlist on a plane while a collaborator edits it online; the device reconnects.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The client pulls changes since its cursor, replays its buffered ops onto the newer version, and both sets of inserts survive (deterministic tie-break on equal ranks). No edit is silently dropped.
failureDrills.mitigation: Operation buffering + sync cursor + versioned, fractional-rank ordered lists.

failureDrills.scenario: A downloaded track is taken down (licensing) while it sits in offline caches.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: On the next online revalidation the offline license for that track is revoked; the local copy becomes unplayable and shows as unavailable, while the rest of the download still plays.
failureDrills.mitigation: Per-track offline license expiry + reconnect revalidation against the rights policy.

steps.title: 6. Play Events: Recommendations and Royalties
description: Every play/skip is a signal. Clients append play events to a durable, partitioned stream — each event carries an event_id (idempotency), a session_id + sequence number, ms_played, and both a client and server timestamp. Events are buffered offline and flushed on reconnect (client_ts ≪ server_ts marks a late/offline play), so offline plays still count.

One stream, several independent consumers with different correctness and latency needs — don't collapse them into one path: recommendations (build per-user features/history → online retrieval → generated playlists), royalty accounting (dedupe by event_id, filter fraud/bot plays, count a play past a threshold as a billable stream, write to an authoritative ledger), fraud filtering, resume-position, and metrics. The stream is at-least-once, so each consumer is idempotent on event_id; the royalty ledger must be exactly-once in effect.

Separating royalty accounting from recommendations matters: recommendations can be eventually consistent and approximate, but the ledger is money and must be deduped, auditable, and reproducible. Recommendations reuse a candidate-gen + ranking funnel over the feature store; generated playlists are written back into the user's library.

view.caption: The client appends play/skip events to the partitioned play event log; one consumer builds features for recommendations (written back to the library), an independent idempotent consumer dedupes events into the royalty ledger.

options.name: Append play events to a stream (default)
options.description: Clients append every play and skip (with listen duration) to a high-throughput log that recommendations, royalty accounting, and metrics each consume independently.
pros: Play/skip events are appended to a high-throughput log, decoupling the playback path from analytics and royalties.
Recommendations, royalty accounting, fraud filtering, and metrics each consume the same stream independently, at their own latency and correctness levels.
A burst of plays on a new release is absorbed by the log instead of overwhelming a database; replayable for backfills and ledger audits.
cons: Consumers see events with some lag — recommendations and counts are eventually consistent.
Operating a durable, partitioned stream and several consumer groups is real infrastructure.
The log is at-least-once, so every consumer must be idempotent on event_id; the royalty ledger must be exactly-once in effect (dedupe + fraud filter before counting).

view.caption: Play/skip events are appended to a partitioned log; a recommendation consumer builds features and writes generated playlists, while an independent idempotent royalty consumer dedupes into the ledger.

options.name: Write each play synchronously to a database
options.description: Each play is written synchronously as a row to a database on the playback path, making counts immediately queryable.
pros: A play is immediately queryable — counts and history are exact with no consumer lag.
No stream infrastructure to run; just an insert per play.
Simple to reason about for low play volumes.
cons: Every play is a synchronous DB write on the playback path, adding latency to start/skip.
A new-release spike of plays becomes a write storm that can overwhelm the database.
Couples playback availability to the analytics DB — if it's slow, playback suffers.

view.caption: Synchronous logging: each play is written straight to the library store via the library service, and the recommendation service reads that history directly.

steps.decisionPrompt: Why route play events through a stream instead of writing each play to the DB synchronously?
whyNow: Play-event capture is both the recommendation fuel and the royalty source of truth; the stream decouples it from serving and downstream compute.

concepts.definition: A durable stream of play/skip events consumed by recs and royalty accounting.
concepts.whyItMatters: Reliable behavior capture (incl. offline) feeding personalization and billing.
concepts.example: Play > 30s -> counts as a stream; history -> Discover Weekly.

recap.after: Durable, deduped play events fanning out to independent recs, royalty-ledger, and resume consumers.
recap.newRisk: Popular tracks/artists create read and event hotspots; ingestion must scale.

flows.name: Play event → stream → recs + royalty ledger
flows.note: Play/skip events stream asynchronously (buffered offline) and fan out to independent consumers — recommendations build features, royalty accounting dedupes into the ledger — never blocking playback.

traps.trap: Counting a billable stream straight off the raw at-least-once log.
traps.why: The log redelivers, clients retry, and bots farm plays — counting naively over-pays royalties and corrupts the ledger.
traps.instead: Dedupe by event_id in a window, filter fraud/bot plays, apply the threshold (e.g. ≥30s), and write idempotently to an auditable ledger so the count is exactly-once in effect.

traps.trap: Putting recommendations and royalty accounting on the same consumer/path.
traps.why: They have opposite requirements — recs tolerate lag and approximation; royalties must be exact, deduped, and reproducible. Coupling them makes one's backlog or bug corrupt the other.
traps.instead: Independent consumer groups off the same stream, each idempotent, scaled and operated separately.

steps.title: 7. Ingestion, Hot Content, and Scale
description: New content enters via an async ingestion/transcode pipeline — a state machine off the serving path, so adding music never affects playback. A master moves through received → transcoding (each bitrate in the ladder) → segmented → published to the catalog, search index, and rights policy. Output keys are deterministic (track+bitrate+segment), so a retry is idempotent and re-running a failed job overwrites the same objects instead of duplicating. Failed jobs go to a dead-letter queue for inspection; publish is the last, atomic step so a track is never half-visible. Rollback unpublishes a bad encode without deleting audio.

Hot content (a viral track, a new release) gets disproportionate reads — absorbed by the CDN (audio) and catalog cache (metadata), with prewarming and an origin shield ahead of an announced drop, and a per-track popularity cache to keep the hottest segments resident. Play-event bursts buffer in the partitioned stream with autoscaled, independent consumers; backpressure is the log's depth, not the playback path.

Shard catalog and library by their access keys; replicate catalog reads and the CDN globally. Audio is durable in object storage; catalog/library/rights/ledger are authoritative; CDN, caches, search index, and feature store are rebuildable derived state. Operate to SLOs (below): start latency, rebuffer ratio, CDN hit ratio, play-log lag, royalty-pipeline lag, and ingestion backlog.

view.caption: At scale the ingestion/transcode pipeline fills the object store and registers tracks in the catalog and search index, while the CDN serves audio and the play event log absorbs high-volume client events.

steps.decisionPrompt: A new album drops and everyone plays it at once. What absorbs the spike?
whyNow: Closing on async ingestion + hot-content scaling shows the design adds content cleanly and survives release spikes.

recap.after: Async ingestion, CDN-absorbed hot reads, scalable event consumers.
recap.newRisk: Licensing-driven catalog changes and royalty accuracy — bounded by re-validation and the durable event log.

bottlenecks.mitigation: Prewarm the CDN before announced drops, origin shield / request collapsing, per-track popularity cache; target ≥98–99% hit ratio.
Partitioned log buffers; autoscale independent rec/royalty consumers; backpressure shows as log lag, not playback latency.

bottlenecks.issue: Ingestion backlog on a big label drop.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Autoscale transcode workers, prioritize imminent releases, deterministic keys for cheap retries; publish stays off the serving path.
Page on start-latency p99 (time-to-first-audio) and rebuffer ratio (stall/play time) breaching target; both surface edge/network or bitrate-adaptation regressions.
Watch CDN hit ratio + origin egress/miss QPS (cost and miss-storm risk) and manifest/entitlement failure rate (broken authorization or rights propagation).
Track play-log consumer lag separately for recs vs royalty, duplicate/fraud-filtered play rate (ledger integrity), offline sync-conflict rate, and ingestion backlog + transcode dead-letter rate.

failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Playback and library are unaffected; recs are slightly stale; events queue durably and catch up.
failureDrills.mitigation: Decoupled event stream; playback never depends on recs.

failureDrills.scenario: A transcode job fails partway (corrupt master or worker crash).
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The job retries idempotently to the same deterministic output keys; after N retries it dead-letters and the track stays unpublished rather than half-visible. Already-published tracks are unaffected.
failureDrills.mitigation: Deterministic output keys, retry + dead-letter, publish as the last atomic step, and rollback that unpublishes without deleting audio.

failureDrills.scenario: A track is taken down for licensing while it's live and in offline caches.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The rights policy bumps the track's version; manifests and cached entitlement are invalidated, new plays are refused, and offline licenses are revoked on next revalidation. Playlists hydrate it as unavailable.
failureDrills.mitigation: Versioned rights policy driving manifest/entitlement invalidation and offline-license revocation (steps 2, 5).

finalDesign.title: Final Design — Audio Streaming
finalDesign.description: An async ingestion pipeline transcodes masters into bitrate segments in object storage and publishes tracks to the catalog, search index, and rights policy. Playback first checks entitlement (subscription, region, availability) via the entitlement/license service, then hands out signed CDN segment URLs and a DRM license for instant-start adaptive streaming. A catalog/library split keeps the shared, cacheable catalog (with a dedicated search index) separate from per-user playlists (item rows referencing tracks, versioned, hydrated on read); offline download issues a device-bound license and caches encrypted audio locally with edits reconciled against a sync cursor on reconnect. Play/skip events stream (buffered offline, deduped by event_id) and fan out to independent consumers — a feature store feeding recommendations and an idempotent royalty consumer writing an auditable ledger. Audio is CDN-served and durable; catalog/library/rights/ledger are authoritative; CDN, caches, search index, and feature store are rebuildable derived state.

view.caption: End to end: clients stream entitlement-checked segments from the CDN via the streaming service, search/browse the catalog, manage playlists in the library service, and feed the play event log that fans out to recommendations (via the feature store) and the royalty ledger — all fed by the ingestion pipeline.

functional.how: Catalog service + dedicated search index for ranked prefix/typo-tolerant search.
Entitlement check -> segmented audio + CDN + first-segment prefetch.
Per-user library, reference-by-id item rows, versioned for concurrent edits.
Device-bound offline license + encrypted local cache + cursor-based library sync.
Play-event stream -> feature store -> recs -> library.

nonFunctional.how: CDN edge for audio; cached catalog.
Durable event stream (offline-buffered), deduped by event_id.
Per-play entitlement check (subscription/region/availability) + DRM/offline licenses + versioned rights policy.
Idempotent, fraud-filtered consumer writing an auditable ledger, isolated from recommendations.

say: Confirm streaming + library + offline + recs over a huge catalog.
Pin instant start and global read scale.
Note play events for recs and royalties.
Segmented audio served via CDN for instant start, with a per-play entitlement check before issuing the manifest.
Catalog/library split, a dedicated search index, and reference-by-id playlist items.
Async ingestion to add content; play-event stream for behavior.
Offline as a state machine: device-bound license, cursor sync, takedown revocation.
Split play-event consumers: feature store -> recs vs idempotent royalty ledger.
Hot-content scaling: CDN prewarm, origin-miss budget, ingestion retries/dead-letter.
Tradeoffs: bitrate choices, offline DRM, royalty-count thresholds.
Mention collaborative playlists and social features as extensions.

expectations: May miss offline sync and play-event pipeline.
Quantifies start-latency/prefetch, CDN hit-ratio + origin-miss budget, and prewarm for drops.
Owns entitlement/DRM, regional availability, takedown invalidation, and offline license expiry/revocation explicitly.
Splits play-event consumers (deduped, fraud-filtered royalty ledger vs eventually-consistent recs) and names the operating SLOs.

followUps: How do you guarantee royalty counts are accurate from (possibly offline) play events?
How would you support collaborative playlists with concurrent edits?
How do you prewarm the CDN for a massive new release?
How do you handle a track becoming unavailable (licensing) while it's in offline caches?
How would you add social features (friends' activity, shared listening)?
